content:
I tend to believe that the best data structure for storing pointers to the DmaFences you want to signal in the dma::FenceCtx would be an XArray.

Reason: you signal fences in order, and you often signal batches. Thus, you want something cache-friendly that gives you a for-each.

Agreed, @Boris Brezillon ?
the other nice property of XArrays is that they can shrink from the bottom without any moves
meaning you can queue new fences with growing seqnos, while older ones are consumed (signalled), and release the memory that was used to store fence pointers with lower seqnos
@Philipp Stanner @Gary Guo @Danilo Krummrich Dunno if this is relevant https://www.kernel.org/doc/html/v6.1/RCU/rcubarrier.html#unloading-modules-that-use-call-rcu
Yeah, I mentioned briefly in the call. I think having that (or synchronize_rcu) called after dropping all fences should be sufficient
Or actually synchronize_rcu is insufficient, it says so explicitly in the doc

"We could try placing a synchronize_rcu() in the module-exit code path, but this is not sufficient. Although synchronize_rcu() does wait for a grace period to elapse, it does not wait for the callbacks to complete."

Could one write a a wait_for_rcu_callbacks() for that scenario?

edit: forget about it, Boris linked that. Exists.
we probably need to do that earlier than module_exit() though
Danilo suggested that it could be called whend dropping FenceCtx
We could also add a small optimization so that for FenceCtx<T> only call it when core::mem::needs_drop::<T>()

Danilo suggested that it could be called whend dropping FenceCtx

yep, that's what I was thinking too
